metadata.title: The Bayes Factor: How Much to Trust a Test
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = Bayes factor / posterior odds / correct interpretation; CRIMSON = false positive / base rate neglect / wrong 90% intuition
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no negative-test BF, no chained updates, no formal Bayes probability formula. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A breast cancer test is 90 percent accurate. You test positive. Most people — including doctors — say that means a 90 percent chance of cancer. The correct answer is closer to 9 percent. One number explains the gap: the Bayes factor.

card.copy: The Bayes Factor: How Much to Trust a Test

beats.narration_text: Posterior odds of disease given a positive test equals prior odds times the Bayes factor. Prior odds: how likely is disease in the population before testing? Bayes factor: how much more likely is a positive test if you are sick versus healthy?

production_viz.label: Posterior odds = Prior odds × Bayes factor equation; three components labeled; TEAL for correct Bayesian framework

beats.narration_text: If the test is 90 percent accurate and you test positive, why is the actual probability of being sick only about 9 percent? What calculation are most people failing to do?

card.copy: Test: 90% accurate. Positive result. Most say 90% chance of disease. Correct answer: ~9%. Where does the gap come from?
card.sub: The base rate is the missing piece

beats.narration_text: Disease prevalence: 1 percent. Sensitivity: 90 percent of sick people test positive. False positive rate: 9 percent of healthy people test positive. Bayes factor equals sensitivity divided by false positive rate: 90 percent over 9 percent equals 10.

production_viz.label: BF = sensitivity/FPR = 90%/9% = 10 calculation; 1% prevalence bar; sensitivity and FPR comparison in TEAL and CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: Prior odds: 1 to 99. Posterior odds: prior times Bayes factor equals 1 to 99 times 10 equals 10 to 99. Probability of disease given positive test: 10 divided by 109, about 9 percent.

production_viz.label: Odds number line: prior 1:99 (CRIMSON) → ×10 Bayes factor → posterior 10:99 → 9% probability (TEAL)

beats.narration_text: The test is genuinely useful — it raised the probability from 1 percent to 9 percent, a ninefold increase. But it is nowhere near 90 percent. The base rate matters enormously. A rare disease means most positive tests are false positives.

production_viz.label: Crowd of 100 people: 1 sick (TEAL), 99 healthy (CRIMSON); after positive test: 10 sick-looking vs 99-9=90... simplified: 10 true positives vs ~9 false positives labeled

beats.narration_text: What makes the test more useful? Either increase the Bayes factor by reducing false positives — or test a higher-risk population where the prior odds are more favorable.

production_viz.label: Two improvement paths: reduce FPR to increase BF (TEAL arrow up) or test high-risk population with better prior (TEAL arrow up); both improve posterior

beats.narration_text: With a 10 to 90 prior instead of 1 to 99: posterior odds equals 100 to 90. Probability of disease given positive: about 53 percent. Same test, same Bayes factor — very different answer because the prior changed.

production_viz.label: High-risk prior 10:90 × BF=10 = 100:90 → 53%; comparison with 1:99 prior → 9%; same BF, different prior in TEAL showing how prior dominates

beats.narration_text: A 2006 study: more than half of practicing gynecologists given these same numbers computed the wrong answer. Base-rate neglect is a systematic error. The Bayes factor framework makes the correct calculation explicit.

production_viz.label: Bar showing >50% of doctors wrong in CRIMSON vs <50% correct in TEAL; 2006 study label; base-rate neglect labeled

beats.narration_text: Bayes factor equals sensitivity over false positive rate. Posterior odds equals prior odds times Bayes factor. Example: 1 percent prevalence, Bayes factor 10, posterior approximately 9 percent, not 90. Prior matters as much as the test.

card.copy: BF = sensitivity / FPR. Posterior odds = prior odds × BF. 1% prevalence, BF=10: posterior ≈ 9%, not 90%. Most doctors get it wrong. Prior is the missing piece.
card.sub: The prior matters as much as the test

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
